preFix.title: Practical zero-waste bathroom product swaps
preFix.subtitle: Which changes make a real difference and which are cosmetic
preFix.excerpt: A typical UK household bathroom uses around 25 to 30 plastic containers per year: shampoo, conditioner, body wash, face wash, toothpaste tubes, razors. Switching to solid bars, refillable packaging, or longer-lasting alternatives eliminates most of that packaging waste. Not all swaps work equally well for every person, this tutorial ranks the swaps by ease, effectiveness, and actual plastic saving.
preFix.sourceNotes: Plastic Free July Foundation UK consumer data 2024. WRAP Household Plastic Survey 2023. BeautyCounter and Ethique product lifecycle analysis data.

content.text: The bathroom generates more single-use plastic packaging per square metre than any other room. Most of it is avoidable without significant compromise to routine or effectiveness. The swaps below are ranked by how easily the product performs to an equivalent standard and how much plastic they eliminate.

Solid shampoo bar, replaces a 250 ml plastic bottle per 50 to 80 washes. One bar lasts roughly as long as 2 to 3 liquid shampoo bottles. Choose a bar with a pH close to scalp pH (4.5 to 5.5) for the cleanest transition, many early solid shampoo bars had a high pH (7 to 8) that left hair feeling waxy; modern formulations have improved significantly. Allow 2 to 3 washes for the scalp to adjust if switching from silicone-containing liquid shampoo.

Safety razor with replaceable steel blades, replaces disposable plastic razors entirely. A quality safety razor lasts indefinitely. Replacement blades are very cheap. Annual running costs are a fraction of disposable cartridges. The learning curve is two to three shaves; the technique differs from cartridge razors but the result is equivalent or better for most users.

Bar soap body wash, a bar of soap replaces a 300 ml body wash bottle approximately every 6 to 8 weeks. The plastic saving is one bottle every 6 to 8 weeks per person. Solid bars last longer if stored on a soap dish that allows them to dry between uses (a wet bar left on a flat surface lasts half as long).

Bamboo toothbrush, eliminates one plastic handle per person per 2 to 3 months. The nylon bristles are not biodegradable; remove them with pliers before composting the handle. Bamboo brushes with plant-based bristles exist but have mixed reviews on cleaning performance.

Toothpaste tablets or powder: these cut out the toothpaste tube, which most UK kerbside schemes cannot recycle. Tablets need a change of technique; powders apply like regular toothpaste. Both clean teeth well if they contain fluoride.
Swaps where the product varies significantly

Solid conditioner bars, effectiveness varies widely between products and hair types. Coarse or very thick hair often needs more conditioning agent per strand than a bar delivers efficiently. If a solid bar leaves hair tangled, it is the wrong product for that hair type, not a failure of the concept.

Menstrual cups and reusable period products, the plastic saving is substantial (a person using tampons or disposable pads creates roughly 100 to 150 plastic-containing items per year). The products work well once the technique is established. The transition period is 1 to 3 cycles.
